text: The Stoic concept of the hegemonikon — the ruling faculty of the mind — depended on solitude's role as a protective enclosure. Marcus Aurelius wrote that a man need not retreat to the mountains or the sea: 'Nowhere can man find a quieter or more untroubled retreat than in his own soul.' This is not mere consolation. Marcus meant it technically: the hegemonikon, when trained, can redirect attention inward faster than any external circumstance can disrupt it. The inner citadel is not a metaphor for patience but a cognitive practice of rapid reorientation.
question: How does Marcus Aurelius describe the inner retreat from a Stoic perspective?

options.text: As a metaphor for patience during hardship
As a cognitive practice of rapid reorientation toward one's own soul
As physical withdrawal to natural settings
As the suppression of emotional responses
